title: Is \(\gamma\approx 0.5024\) the optimal success probability for the random-order single-sample secretary (two-sided Game of Googol)?
latex:
Given an integer \(n\ge 1\), let \(D_1,\dots,D_n\) be arbitrary (unknown to the decision maker) probability distributions on \(\mathbb{R}\). Independently for each \(i\in\{1,\dots,n\}\), draw two independent samples \(X_i\sim D_i\) and \(Y_i\sim D_i\). The values \(Y_1,\dots,Y_n\) are revealed to the decision maker before any \(X\)-value is revealed.

Next, a uniformly random permutation \(\pi\) of \(\{1,\dots,n\}\) is drawn, and the values \(X_{\pi(1)},X_{\pi(2)},\dots,X_{\pi(n)}\) are revealed sequentially in that order. After seeing \(X_{\pi(t)}\), the decision maker must either accept it and stop, or reject it irrevocably and continue. Formally, an algorithm is a stopping time \(\tau\in\{1,\dots,n\}\) adapted to the filtration generated by \((Y_1,\dots,Y_n)\) and \((X_{\pi(1)},\dots,X_{\pi(t)})\) up to time \(t\), and it outputs the selected value \(X_{\pi(\tau)}\).

The success event is selecting the overall maximum among the \(X\)-values:
\[
\text{Success} \;:=\; \{X_{\pi(\tau)} = \max_{1\le i\le n} X_i\}.
\]
Define the worst-case (distribution-free) success probability of an algorithm \(\mathcal{A}\) as
\[
\mathrm{Succ}(\mathcal{A}) \;:=\; \inf_{n\ge 1}\;\inf_{D_1,\dots,D_n}\; \Pr[\text{Success}],
\]
where the probability is over the draws of \((X_i,Y_i)\), the random permutation \(\pi\), and any internal randomness of \(\mathcal{A}\).

Let
\[
\mathrm{OPT} \;:=\; \sup_{\mathcal{A}} \mathrm{Succ}(\mathcal{A}).
\]
Let \(\gamma\) denote the optimal (limit) success probability in the classical random-order secretary variant “choosing the best of the current crop” with \(\alpha=1/2\): \(n\) distinct values arrive in uniformly random order, the first \(\lfloor n/2\rfloor\) values may only be observed (cannot be accepted), and one must select the maximum among the remaining values; \(\gamma\approx 0.5024\) is the supremum success probability in the limit \(n\to\infty\) (in the comparison model).

problem: Determine \(\mathrm{OPT}\) for the random-order single-sample secretary problem (equivalently, the random-order two-sided Game of Googol). In particular, prove or refute that
\[
\mathrm{OPT} = \gamma \approx 0.5024.
\]
Equivalently, either
- give an algorithm \(\mathcal{A}\) with \(\mathrm{Succ}(\mathcal{A})>\gamma\), or
- prove that every algorithm \(\mathcal{A}\) satisfies \(\mathrm{Succ}(\mathcal{A})\le \gamma\).
context: Explicitly stated in Section 1.1 (Our contributions), immediately after Theorem 2: “We believe that the factor of \(\gamma\) should be the optimal answer for the two-sided Game of Googol, as well as the single sample secretary problem; we leave this as an problem.”

sources.theorem_or_result:
The following results are stated for the random-order single-sample secretary problem (equivalently, the random-order two-sided Game of Googol):
- There exists an algorithm with worst-case success probability at least approximately \(0.5009\).
- In the comparison model (algorithms using only pairwise comparisons), no algorithm can achieve worst-case success probability strictly larger than \(\gamma \approx 0.5024\); this upper bound is obtained by restricting to an i.i.d. special case that reduces to a previously solved “training sample / current crop” best-choice problem.
- Under a “no-superstars” regularity condition (no single \(D_i\) is too likely to produce the overall maximum), there exists an algorithm whose success probability approaches \(\gamma\) asymptotically.
The paper also proves that in adversarial order, the optimal success probability is \(1/4\) (tight).
sources.connection: This paper is the closest known progress toward determining \(\mathrm{OPT}\) in the stated model. It improves the best known lower bound for random order from \(0.45292\) to about \(0.5009\), leaving only a small gap to the canonical upper bound \(\gamma\approx 0.5024\). It also identifies the source of the \(0.5024\) barrier in the comparison model by linking to an older best-choice problem with a training sample. The remaining open question aligns exactly with whether the comparison-model upper bound \(\gamma\) is information-theoretically tight for the full distribution-free model.

  journal = {Journal of Machine Learning Research},

  number  = {113},

  note    = {Preliminary version in SODA 2020; arXiv:1907.06001},

}
sources.theorem_or_result:
For the secretary objective in the two-sided Game of Googol, the paper proves:
- For any instance, the closed moving window algorithm \(\mathrm{ALG}_c\) satisfies
  \[\Pr[\mathrm{ALG}_c = \max D] \ge \ln 2\,\Bigl(1-\tfrac{\ln 2}{2}\Bigr) \approx 0.45292,\]
  where \(D\) denotes the multiset of hidden-side values.
The paper also establishes prophet-type (expected-value) guarantees, but the above is the main probability-of-selecting-the-maximum guarantee.
sources.connection: This work introduces and analyzes the two-sided Game of Googol model that is equivalent (under independent sampling on the two sides) to the random-order single-sample secretary problem in the prompt. Its \(0.45292\) lower bound was the prior state of the art later improved by Nuti--Vondr{\'{a}}k. The techniques (moving-window style rules and combinatorial symmetries between samples and realizations) are part of the methodological backbone for later improvements. It also supplies a baseline lower bound for \(\mathrm{OPT}\) in the exact model of interest.

sources.theorem_or_result:
A best-choice problem with partial information is studied: a training sample of size \(m\) is observed first, and then \(n\) additional items arrive sequentially, with decisions based on relative ranks among all \(m+n\) observations.
- Optimal stopping rules depend essentially only on \(m+n\), and if \(m/(m+n)\to t\in(0,1)\), then the optimal success probability \(P^*(m,n)\) converges to an explicitly derived limit \(p^*(t)\).
- In particular, for \(t=1/2\) (equal-size training and online phases), the limit value \(p^*(1/2)\) is approximately \(0.50\) (later works in the algorithms literature quote \(p^*(1/2)\approx 0.5024\)).
sources.connection: The i.i.d. specialization of the single-sample secretary problem (all \(D_i\) identical and continuous) reduces to a training-sample best-choice problem of the type analyzed here, with \(m=n\). The resulting optimal probability \(p^*(1/2)\) is the source of the constant \(\gamma\approx 0.5024\) that serves as an upper bound for comparison-based algorithms in the single-sample secretary setting. Thus this paper is a key ingredient in establishing the best-known upper bound barrier and in formulating the conjecture \(\mathrm{OPT}=\gamma\).

}
sources.theorem_or_result:
The arXiv abstract states that the paper develops a factor-revealing linear program that captures secretary variants with advice (including advice in the form of samples), and gives tight analyses of optimal algorithms in these models.
The exact theorem statements for the “secretaries with samples” subproblem were not accessible in full text during this search session; consequently, only the high-level claim can be verified here: the paper provides an LP-based characterization and tight bounds for success probabilities in sample-advice secretary models.
sources.connection: This work is one of the main modern rediscoveries/LP-formulations cited as re-establishing classical constants (including the \(\gamma\approx 0.5024\) barrier) for secretary problems with sample-type advice. Even though it does not directly settle \(\mathrm{OPT}\) for the two-sided Game of Googol, it provides a systematic optimization framework (factor-revealing LP) that can be used to derive sharp upper bounds for restricted policy classes. Its methodology is directly relevant to attempts to prove that \(\mathrm{Succ}(\mathcal A)\le \gamma\) for all algorithms in the single-sample model.

sources.theorem_or_result:
This paper studies a model where each of \(n\) adversarially chosen values is independently designated as a “sample/prior” with probability \(p\) (observed before the online phase) and otherwise remains in the online sequence arriving in random order; the goal is to maximize the probability of selecting the maximum online value.
The exact theorem statements (including the optimal success probability as a function of \(p\), and the \(p=1/2\) constant) were not accessible in full text during this search session; only the problem definition and existence of optimal algorithms/bounds can be verified via bibliographic records and secondary descriptions.
sources.connection: The independent-sampling model is closely related to the “choosing the best of the current crop” / training-sample formulation that yields the constant \(\gamma\approx 0.5024\) when half the instance is sample-like information. This is the same constant that appears as an upper bound in the random-order single-sample secretary problem under comparison-based algorithms. Techniques from this paper (and its LP/continuous-limit analyses in follow-up work) are natural candidates for extending upper bounds beyond the comparison model or for proving tightness via matching strategies.

sources.theorem_or_result:
In a model where each item is independently “sampled” (observed only in a prior phase) with probability \(p\), the paper formulates a linear program for optimal stopping with random arrival order and analyzes its continuous limit.
- A structural result (as stated in the arXiv abstract) identifies an optimal algorithm described by a nondecreasing sequence of thresholds \(t_1\le t_2\le\cdots\), where the decision maker should stop on an item of current relative rank \(i\) if it appears after time \(t_i\).
The abstract also indicates that classic secretary/minimum-rank results can be recovered within this framework.
sources.connection: Although the objective in this paper is more general than “select the maximum with highest probability”, it develops LP and continuous-limit tools for random-order optimal stopping with partial prior observations, a setting conceptually adjacent to the single-sample secretary problem. The characterization of optimal policies by rank-dependent time thresholds parallels strategies used in two-sided Googol algorithms. These analytical tools may help convert the conjecture \(\mathrm{OPT}=\gamma\) into a tractable optimization problem or provide sharper upper bounds by capturing the best possible behavior of broad policy classes.

  booktitle = {11th Innovations in Theoretical Computer Science Conference (ITCS 2020)},
  series    = {Leibniz International Proceedings in Informatics (LIPIcs)},
  volume    = {151},
  pages     = {60:1--60:10},
  year      = {2020},
  publisher = {Schloss Dagstuhl--Leibniz-Zentrum f{\"{u}}r Informatik},
  doi       = {10.4230/LIPIcs.ITCS.2020.60}
}
sources.theorem_or_result:
For the classic single-choice prophet inequality with known arrival order (maximize expected value), but where the decision maker has access only to one independent sample from each distribution \(D_i\):
- There exists a stopping rule achieving competitive ratio \(1/2\) with respect to \(\mathbb{E}[\max_i X_i]\) using only the samples.
- The factor \(1/2\) is optimal.
(These statements are widely cited and are also described in later papers’ introductions; the ITCS paper is the standard reference.)
sources.connection: This paper is central background for understanding “single-sample” power in online selection, but for an expected-value (prophet) objective rather than probability-of-choosing-the-maximum. It demonstrates that in some stopping problems, one sample per distribution suffices to recover the optimal constant achievable with full distribution knowledge. The random-order single-sample secretary problem in the prompt is a contrasting regime: Nuti--Vondr{\'{a}}k show the best known constant appears to be around \(0.5\), strictly below the i.i.d. full-information best-choice constant \(\approx 0.5801\). This contrast motivates why determining \(\mathrm{OPT}\) and the role of \(\gamma\approx 0.5024\) are nontrivial.

sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper studies maximizing \(\Pr[\text{select }\max_i X_i]\) when values come from independent distributions \(D_i\) that are known (full distributional knowledge) and arrive online.
- In the fixed (adversarial) arrival order model with known \(D_i\), there is an algorithm with success probability \(1/e\), and \(1/e\) is tight.
- In the random arrival order model with known \(D_i\), static-threshold algorithms can beat \(1/e\), and more general multi-threshold algorithms do strictly better than any single-threshold algorithm.
- Under a “no-superstars” condition, the optimal achievable success probability in the random-order case matches the i.i.d. case (approximately \(0.5801\)).
sources.connection: This paper analyzes the same objective (probability of selecting the maximum) but in a model with full distribution knowledge rather than single samples. Its “no-superstars” condition and Poissonization-style analyses are conceptually aligned with later no-superstars results in the single-sample setting (where \(\gamma\approx 0.5024\) becomes attainable). It clarifies that even with full knowledge, random order can fundamentally change the achievable success probability relative to adversarial order. These comparisons contextualize why the single-sample random-order constant might plausibly equal a training-sample constant \(\gamma\approx 0.5024\), and why proving \(\mathrm{OPT}=\gamma\) likely requires new ideas beyond those used for the full-information case.
